title: China, the US, and Nuclear Energy Geopolitics in Southeast Asia
gist: Nuclear reactor exports, especially small modular reactors, have become a central arena of US-China strategic competition in Southeast Asia, where countries are hedging by pairing US technology/regulatory credibility with Chinese cost and speed advantages and South Korean/Japanese construction and manufacturing capacity.

themes.theme: Nuclear exports as the new strategic competition arena

insights.claim: China's 15th Five-Year Plan sectoral documents (Beautiful China plan and New-type Energy System plan, published by June 2026) reveal an ambition to become a global leader in nuclear energy governance and reactor exports, not just domestic nuclear build-out.
insights.speaker: Yu-Hsuan Yeh
insights.so_what: Track Chinese state nuclear firms (CNNC, CGN) for new export deals as a leading indicator of Beijing's nuclear diplomacy execution against its stated 15th FYP ambitions.
insights.locator: After launching its 15th Five-Year Plan (FYP) in March

insights.claim: The United States, Japan, and South Korea announced a new Memorandum of Cooperation at the NATO Summit to accelerate deployment of small modular reactors (SMRs) in third countries, starting with the Indo-Pacific, almost simultaneously with China's five-year plan releases.
insights.speaker: Yu-Hsuan Yeh
insights.so_what: A trilateral US-Japan-South Korea SMR export coalition is a direct competitive response to China's nuclear export ambitions; expect coordinated bids in Southeast Asia and possibly other Indo-Pacific markets.
insights.locator: Almost simultaneously, the United States, Japan, and South Korea

insights.claim: Since the Trump administration took office, the US has explicitly linked domestic and international nuclear reactor deployment to national security, with the '123 Agreement' for peaceful nuclear cooperation as the key indicator of US nuclear energy diplomacy.
insights.speaker: Yu-Hsuan Yeh
insights.so_what: New 123 Agreement signings are a trackable proxy for expanding US nuclear export influence; count of signatories in Southeast Asia is a concrete leading metric.
insights.locator: Since the Trump administration took office

insights.claim: About 60 percent of Southeast Asia's crude oil passes through the Strait of Hormuz, and its closure has already forced some regional governments to roll out short-term energy demand curbs.
insights.speaker: Yu-Hsuan Yeh
insights.so_what: Heavy Hormuz dependency is a structural vulnerability accelerating Southeast Asian governments' urgency to diversify into nuclear power; any further Strait disruption would intensify nuclear procurement timelines.
insights.locator: Energy security is a major vulnerability for Southeast Asia

insights.claim: Southeast Asia's electricity demand is projected to grow by more than 100 TWh by 2030, driven by data centers, electric vehicles, and industrial needs, necessitating enhanced energy supply and security.
insights.speaker: Yu-Hsuan Yeh
insights.so_what: Data center and EV-driven demand growth is the structural tailwind underpinning Southeast Asia's nuclear ambitions — a proxy for regional power infrastructure and grid investment opportunity.
insights.locator: Southeast Asia's electricity demand is projected to grow

insights.claim: Indonesia, the Philippines, and Vietnam have set national targets to operate their first nuclear reactors before 2035, while Malaysia, Myanmar, Singapore, and Thailand are actively considering nuclear energy, with interest concentrated specifically on SMRs.
insights.speaker: Yu-Hsuan Yeh
insights.so_what: A pre-2035 nuclear buildout target across three Southeast Asian countries plus active interest from four more defines a concrete, dated pipeline of SMR procurement decisions to monitor.
insights.locator: some countries, including Indonesia, the Philippines and Vietnam

themes.theme: Construction track record: Asia beats the West

insights.claim: In the US and Europe, new-built nuclear reactors have been rare in recent years, with licensing processes and loss of construction expertise prolonging project timelines and raising costs, whereas most newly built reactors globally are constructed by Asian countries, mainly China and South Korea, which have proven ability to build on time and on budget.
insights.speaker: Yu-Hsuan Yeh
insights.so_what: China and South Korea's demonstrated on-time, on-budget construction track record is their key competitive edge over the US/Europe in winning Southeast Asian nuclear contracts — a durable execution moat, not just financing.
insights.locator: Nuclear reactors are often viewed as having high upfront costs

insights.claim: Operating a nuclear power plant in a new country takes roughly 10-15 years to build out full supporting infrastructure per the IAEA, and given reactor lifespans of 60-80 years plus 10-25 years for decommissioning, importing a reactor implies roughly 100 years of political, logistical, educational, and legal alignment with the exporting country.
insights.speaker: Yu-Hsuan Yeh
insights.so_what: A ~100-year alignment horizon means nuclear reactor export deals function as multi-generational geopolitical commitments, not simple infrastructure sales — a key reason competing powers are fighting hard for first-mover contracts now.
insights.locator: The process, according to the International Atomic Energy Agency

themes.theme: Russia, China, and US export models compared

insights.claim: Russia leads globally in the number of reactors exported via state-owned Rosatom, whose build-own-operate model and ability to cover the entire fuel cycle are attractive to new nuclear countries, but sanctions following the invasion of Ukraine have significantly hurt its financing and logistics, causing construction delays.
insights.speaker: Yu-Hsuan Yeh
insights.so_what: Sanctions-driven delays are eroding Rosatom's competitive position, creating an opening that China, South Korea, and the US-led coalition are racing to fill in Southeast Asia.
insights.locator: Russia, China, and the United States are the major nuclear reactor exporters

insights.claim: Although China was once set to build 30 reactors in Belt and Road Initiative countries by 2030 via CNNC and CGN, it has so far only built reactors in Pakistan, where Chinese companies don't own or operate the reactors but fuel supply remains dependent on China.
insights.speaker: Yu-Hsuan Yeh
insights.so_what: China's actual nuclear export delivery (Pakistan only) has fallen far short of its stated 30-reactor BRI target, indicating execution risk behind Beijing's export ambitions despite its strong domestic construction record.
insights.locator: Although China was once set to build 30 reactors

insights.claim: US nuclear energy exports are private-sector driven, but the government supports importing countries via programs including FIRST (capacity building focused on security/safety/nonproliferation), EXIM's SMR Financing Toolkit, and DFC loans/guaranties/investments meant to offset the high upfront investment burden; the US does not take back spent fuel for export, though fuel recycling is gaining policy traction.
insights.speaker: Yu-Hsuan Yeh
insights.so_what: The layered US financing toolkit (FIRST, EXIM, DFC) is designed specifically to neutralize China's cost advantage for importing countries — watch DFC/EXIM deal announcements as a proxy for US nuclear export momentum.
insights.locator: The private sector has driven U.S. nuclear energy exports

insights.claim: Among Southeast Asian countries exploring nuclear energy, Indonesia, the Philippines, Vietnam, Singapore, and Thailand have all signed 123 Agreements with the United States, while Malaysia has secured an MoU to begin negotiations, reflecting a preference for US technology, regulatory framework, and safety culture even as China's cost and speed advantages remain attractive.
insights.speaker: Yu-Hsuan Yeh
insights.so_what: Widespread 123 Agreement signings give the US a regulatory/trust head start in Southeast Asia, but this doesn't guarantee contract wins given China's cost and delivery-speed edge — the real competition is at the financing/construction-bid stage.
insights.locator: Among Southeast Asian countries exploring nuclear energy

themes.theme: China's ASEAN nuclear engagement and the Linglong One question mark

insights.claim: China began civil nuclear cooperation with ASEAN in 2015 through state-owned nuclear companies to build workforce capacity, and after its Linglong One became the world's first IAEA-approved land-based SMR model, China demonstrated its ability to serve BRI countries, which include all ASEAN members.
insights.speaker: Yu-Hsuan Yeh
insights.so_what: The Linglong One's IAEA approval is China's flagship credential for SMR exports to ASEAN — a first-mover technical validation that could shape Southeast Asian SMR procurement even before commercial operation is proven.
insights.locator: China began civil nuclear cooperation with the Association of Southeast Asian Nations

insights.claim: Despite leading the world in SMR progress, China's Linglong One has missed its commercial operation timeline, and due to information opacity, whether it can operate successfully remains a key open question for countries weighing nuclear energy options.
insights.speaker: Yu-Hsuan Yeh
insights.so_what: A missed commercial-operation deadline for China's flagship SMR is a concrete execution risk that Southeast Asian buyers should weigh against China's cost/speed pitch — watch for the actual Linglong One operational milestone as a key signal for China's SMR export credibility.
insights.locator: However, despite leading the world in SMR progress

themes.theme: US regulatory inroads and the South Korea/Japan construction-capacity bridge

insights.claim: In 2025, US nuclear company Thorcon International received a first-stage license from Indonesia's nuclear regulator BAPETEN for its molten salt reactor, while USTDA funded a Meralco study on integrating US SMRs in the Philippines and the US Department of Commerce set up an SMR export working group to assist industry communications with Southeast Asian governments.
insights.speaker: Yu-Hsuan Yeh
insights.so_what: Concrete US regulatory wins (Thorcon's BAPETEN license, Meralco study) mark tangible progress converting US policy tools into commercial traction in Indonesia and the Philippines — early proof points for the US export strategy.
insights.locator: The United States has made some regulatory breakthroughs in Southeast Asia

insights.claim: The US still has to overcome a construction bottleneck to appeal to Southeast Asian countries, and that's where South Korea and Japan play key roles: South Korea's state-owned KHNP built four large reactors in the UAE on schedule, winning further deals in Egypt and Czechia plus SMR cooperation MOUs with Singapore, the Philippines (Meralco), and Thailand.
insights.speaker: Yu-Hsuan Yeh
insights.so_what: South Korea's KHNP is emerging as the construction-execution backbone of the US-aligned nuclear export coalition, given its proven on-schedule UAE track record — a company to watch for further Southeast Asian SMR contract wins.
insights.locator: Despite the regulatory integration, the United States still has to overcome

insights.claim: Japan Steel Works supplies 80 percent of the world's large forged components for nuclear reactors, and leveraging Japan's manufacturing capabilities to rebuild the US nuclear energy industry was a key focus of the 2025 US-Japan trade deal.
insights.speaker: Yu-Hsuan Yeh
insights.so_what: Japan Steel Works' 80% global share of large forged nuclear components is a critical supply-chain chokepoint — any disruption there would bottleneck the entire US-aligned nuclear export coalition, not just Japan's own projects.
insights.locator: Japan is a key supplier to the nuclear energy industry

themes.theme: China's next-phase strategy: energy-system integration before nuclear

insights.claim: Since the release of the 15th FYP, China National Nuclear Corporation (CNNC) has signed new cooperation agreements with China Energy Engineering Corporation (CEEC, an overseas energy project developer operating in 140 countries and regions) and TBEA (a renewable energy and transmission solutions provider), citing international market expansion in all of them, indicating a strategy of integrating into other countries' energy systems first, then advancing the nuclear development agenda.
insights.speaker: Yu-Hsuan Yeh
insights.so_what: China's sequenced strategy of broad energy-system integration ahead of nuclear-specific deals is a longer-horizon competitive approach investors should track via CEEC/TBEA overseas energy project wins as leading indicators of future CNNC nuclear deals.
insights.locator: Since the release of the 15 FYP, CNNC has signed new cooperation agreements

insights.claim: Southeast Asia has historically cooperated with China on economic agendas and with the United States on security, and the region must now navigate nuclear energy cooperation with both sides while maintaining this balanced position amid heightened geopolitical competition, a global energy crisis, and rapid domestic energy demand growth.
insights.speaker: Yu-Hsuan Yeh
insights.so_what: Southeast Asian nuclear procurement will likely be split/hedged across US and Chinese suppliers rather than exclusive, meaning investors should expect multi-vendor nuclear pipelines in the region rather than a single winner-take-all market.
insights.locator: Southeast Asia has historically cooperated with China on economic agendas
